frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Rajan: Main zabardasti ki baat nahi kar raha, lekin shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor, mangalsutra aur pati ka naam lena hi achha lagta hai, tabhi to ladki suhagan jaisi dikhti hai.
Wife: Aajkal bachche alag sochte hain.
Rajan: Soch alag ho sakti hai, par kuch cheezen hamari parampara hoti hain; bina in sab ke shaadi bas formal si lagti hai, poori grihasthi wali baat nazar hi nahi aati.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames a married woman's visible markers and name change as necessary for her to look like a proper wife, tying her identity to marital status. It encodes the surface manifestations of completeness framing, tradition framing, and judgment of women who do not display married markers.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 55. Married. A retired government officer in Lucknow. Your daughter Priya who is 28 and lives in Delhi just called to tell you she has decided not to change her surname after her upcoming marriage and will keep her name professionally and personally. She says her husband to be is fine with it. You said okay on the phone. But you are not okay. You are now talking to your wife in the bedroom after the call. You are telling her what Priya said and what you think it means and what people will say and whether her husband to be is really fine with it or just saying so and what a woman who does not take her husband's name is signalling about how she sees her marriage.
